skill.name: firebase-crashlytics
skill.description: Comprehensive guide for Firebase Crashlytics, including provisioning and SDK usage. Use this skill when the user needs help setting up Crashlytics, adding crash reporting, or using the Crashlytics SDK in their application.
skill.summary: Step-by-step Firebase Crashlytics setup guide for Android and iOS with SDK integration, Gradle and Xcode configuration, and crash verification.
skill.author: firebase

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged 82 potential issues: 65 external command patterns from markdown backtick formatting of Firebase CLI examples in documentation, 7 hardcoded URLs to official Google/Firebase domains, and 10 false positive blocker detections on normal documentation text. After manual evaluation, all 82 findings are confirmed false positives. The skill is a documentation-only setup guide from Google's Firebase team with no executable code, no credential handling, and no data exfiltration. All URLs point to trusted firebase.google.com, maven.google.com, and github.com/firebase domains.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Blocks Contain Shell Command Examples
low_findings.description: Documentation files contain shell command examples using npx -y firebase-tools@latest formatted in markdown code blocks. These are instructional code snippets, not executable scripts. The commands use the official Firebase CLI tool and pose no security risk.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs to Official Firebase Documentation
low_findings.description: Multiple reference URLs point to firebase.google.com, maven.google.com, and github.com/firebase for SDK documentation and package repositories. All domains belong to Google/Firebase and are trusted. No user data is transmitted to these URLs by the skill.

dangerous_patterns.title: Markdown Backtick Formatting Flagged as Shell Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The static analyzer incorrectly classified markdown inline code backticks and fenced code blocks as Ruby/shell backtick execution. The skill files contain Firebase CLI command examples (npx -y firebase-tools@latest) embedded in markdown documentation as code snippets. No backtick execution occurs; these are purely instructional code examples rendered in markdown code formatting.

dangerous_patterns.title: Documentation Reference URLs Flagged as Network Indicators
dangerous_patterns.description: The static analyzer flagged legitimate documentation reference URLs as hardcoded network destinations. These URLs point to firebase.google.com/docs (official documentation), maven.google.com (Google Maven artifact repository), and github.com/firebase (official Firebase GitHub organization). No data exfiltration or unauthorized network requests occur.

content.user_title: Automate Firebase Crashlytics Setup and Integration
content.value_statement: Setting up Firebase Crashlytics crash reporting requires multiple platform-specific configuration steps that are easy to miss or misconfigure. This skill guides Claude through complete Android and iOS Crashlytics setup, from project provisioning and SDK integration to test crash verification.
actual_capabilities: Guide Android Crashlytics setup with Gradle plugin configuration and Google Services integration
Guide iOS Crashlytics setup with Swift Package Manager and Xcode build phase configuration
Provide platform-specific code snippets for initializing the Crashlytics SDK
Explain how to force a test crash and verify crash reports appear in the Firebase console
Reference custom crash report options including custom keys, logs, and user identifiers
Link to official Firebase documentation for advanced Crashlytics customization features
limitations: Cannot directly execute Firebase CLI commands or modify project files on the user's system
Does not cover Firebase project creation or authentication setup, which require the firebase-basics skill
Only covers Android and iOS platforms; does not support Flutter, Unity, or web Crashlytics setup
Cannot read actual crash reports from the Firebase console or MCP server

use_cases.title: Android Developer Adding Crash Reporting
use_cases.description: An Android developer needs to add Crashlytics to their existing app. The skill guides them through Gradle plugin setup, SDK dependency management, and google-services.json configuration.

use_cases.title: iOS Developer Integrating Crash Monitoring
use_cases.description: An iOS developer wants to monitor app crashes. The skill helps with Swift Package Manager installation, Xcode Run Script phases for dSYM upload, and Firebase initialization in app code.

use_cases.title: New Team Member Learning Firebase Tools
use_cases.description: A developer new to Firebase needs a structured walkthrough of Crashlytics setup and verification. The skill provides step-by-step platform guides with test crash instructions.

prompt_templates.title: Start Android Crashlytics Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me add Firebase Crashlytics to my Android project. I already have a Firebase project set up.
prompt_templates.scenario: Initial Android Crashlytics integration with Gradle configuration

prompt_templates.title: Start iOS Crashlytics Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to integrate Crashlytics into my iOS app using Swift Package Manager. Walk me through all the steps.
prompt_templates.scenario: iOS Crashlytics integration with SPM and Xcode configuration

prompt_templates.title: Verify Crash Reporting Is Working
prompt_templates.prompt: I have added the Crashlytics SDK to my app. Show me how to force a test crash and confirm it appears in the Firebase console.
prompt_templates.scenario: Testing Crashlytics setup with a test crash and Firebase console verification

prompt_templates.title: Configure Native Crash Support for Android
prompt_templates.prompt: My Android app uses native C++ code. Help me configure Crashlytics NDK support to capture and symbolicate native crashes.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced NDK crash reporting setup with native symbol upload

output_examples.input: I need to set up Crashlytics for my Android app
output_examples.output: To set up Crashlytics on Android, you need to add the Crashlytics Gradle plugin and SDK dependency. First, add the plugin to your project-level build.gradle.kts, then add the dependency and plugin to your app-level build.gradle.kts. I will guide you through each file change with the exact Gradle configuration code needed.

output_examples.input: How do I verify Crashlytics is working after setup?
output_examples.output: After integrating the SDK, force a test crash by adding a delayed RuntimeException in your main activity. Run the app, let it crash, then restart it. The crash report will appear in the Firebase console under Crashlytics within a few minutes. Remember to remove the test crash code after verification is complete.

output_examples.input: My iOS app has native code. How do I configure dSYM upload?
output_examples.output: For iOS, you need to add a Run Script build phase in Xcode that runs the Crashlytics upload script. Also ensure your Debug Information Format is set to DWARF with dSYM File in Build Settings. If you use the xcode-project-setup skill, these steps are handled automatically when adding the FirebaseCrashlytics package.

best_practices: Complete all prerequisite steps in the firebase-basics skill before starting Crashlytics setup
Always remove test crash code from your app after verifying that crash reports appear in the Firebase console
Use the Firebase Bill of Materials to manage SDK versions consistently across all Firebase dependencies
anti_patterns: Do not leave test crash code in production builds after successful verification
Do not skip the Google Services configuration step when setting up Android Crashlytics
Do not add Crashlytics SDK dependencies without a corresponding registered Firebase project and app

faq.question: What platforms does this skill support?
faq.answer: This skill covers Android and iOS platform setup for Firebase Crashlytics. It does not cover Flutter, Unity, or web platforms.

faq.question: Do I need a Firebase project before using this skill?
faq.answer: Yes. You need an existing Firebase project and a registered Android or iOS app. Use the firebase-basics skill for project and app setup first.

faq.question: How do I verify Crashlytics is working after setup?
faq.answer: Add a test crash in your app code, run the app, let it crash, then restart it. After a few minutes, check the Firebase console Crashlytics dashboard for the crash report.

faq.question: Can this skill read crash reports from Firebase?
faq.answer: No. This skill only guides setup and integration. To read crash reports, use the Firebase MCP server or view them directly in the Firebase console.

faq.question: Does this skill support native crash reporting?
faq.answer: Yes for Android. The skill includes instructions for configuring Crashlytics NDK to capture and symbolicate native C and C++ crashes in Android apps.

faq.question: Is the Firebase CLI required to use this skill?
faq.answer: The Firebase CLI is recommended but not required. The skill can guide you through setup steps using the Firebase console as an alternative.
